agent.system_template: You are an expert software engineer debugging C/C++ programs. You¥ninteract with a Unix shell via two tools ¥u2014 `bash` for general shell¥ncommands and `gdb` for stateful debugger commands against the buggy¥nbinary.¥n
agent.instance_template: {{task}}¥n¥n<instructions>¥n# Task Instructions¥n¥nYou're investigating a bug in a C/C++ program. Two tools are¥navailable:¥n¥n  bash   stateless shell ¥u2014 `ls`, `cat`, `grep`, `nl`, `file`, etc.¥n         Each call is a fresh subshell. Useful for source inspection.¥n¥n  gdb    stateful gdb session pre-loaded with the buggy binary.¥n         The session persists across calls ¥u2014 `break`, `run`, `bt`,¥n         `print`, `step`, `continue`, `frame N`, etc. retain state¥n         from one call to the next. The binary and its argv are¥n         already configured; just `run` to start.¥n¥nUse `gdb` for ANY debugger work ¥u2014 it's ‾10x more useful than¥n`gdb -batch` from bash because state persists.¥n¥nFor each response:¥n1. Include reasoning text explaining your analysis.¥n2. Provide ONE OR MORE tool calls (`bash` and/or `gdb`).¥n¥n## Workflow¥n¥n1. `bash`: read the source (`nl -ba program.c`).¥n2. `gdb`: `run` to observe the crash; `bt` for backtrace.¥n3. `gdb`: `frame N` / `print VAR` / `info locals` to inspect state.¥n4. Iterate as needed ¥u2014 `gdb` keeps your breakpoints / current frame.¥n5. Form a diagnosis grounded in the evidence.¥n¥n3-8 investigation steps is typical.¥n¥n## CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS¥n¥n- Every response MUST include AT LEAST ONE tool call (`bash` or¥n  `gdb`). A response with no tool calls is rejected.¥n- Use non-interactive flags. No editors / pagers (`vi`, `less`).¥n- Each `bash` call is a fresh subshell; `cd` and `export` don't¥n  persist between bash calls. The `gdb` session, by contrast, IS¥n  stateful.¥n¥n## Submission¥n¥nWhen done investigating, your final response MUST include all three¥nlabelled paragraphs in the THOUGHT text BEFORE the submit `bash` call:¥n¥n  ROOT CAUSE: <file:line and what is wrong, in your own words.>¥n  LOCAL FIX:  <minimal code change that resolves the symptom.>¥n  GLOBAL FIX: <structural change preventing this CLASS of bug ¥u2014¥n              type changes, API redesign, compile-time check, etc.>¥n¥nThen submit using:¥n¥n    bash tool: {¥

}¥n¥nThe judge reads your THOUGHT prose. If you submit without writing the¥nthree labelled sections, your run scores 0.¥n¥n<system_information>¥n{{system}} {{release}} {{machine}}¥n</system_information>¥n</instructions>¥n

messages.content: You are an expert software engineer debugging C/C++ programs. You¥ninteract with a Unix shell via two tools ¥u2014 `bash` for general shell¥ncommands and `gdb` for stateful debugger commands against the buggy¥nbinary.

You're debugging a C/C++ bug. The buggy binary is at `./build/prog` and the source file is `program.c` in the current directory.¥n¥nRun command: `./build/prog `¥n¥nExpected behavior: crashes when run (likely a sanitizer report or signal).¥n¥nYou have a stateful gdb session pre-loaded with `./build/prog` (argv already configured). Use `gdb` for runtime debugging, `bash` for source inspection. Identify the root cause and propose both a local fix and a structural global fix.¥n¥n¥n<instructions>¥n# Task Instructions¥n¥nYou're investigating a bug in a C/C++ program. Two tools are¥navailable:¥n¥n  bash   stateless shell ¥u2014 `ls`, `cat`, `grep`, `nl`, `file`, etc.¥n         Each call is a fresh subshell. Useful for source inspection.¥n¥n  gdb    stateful gdb session pre-loaded with the buggy binary.¥n         The session persists across calls ¥u2014 `break`, `run`, `bt`,¥n         `print`, `step`, `continue`, `frame N`, etc. retain state¥n         from one call to the next. The binary and its argv are¥n         already configured; just `run` to start.¥n¥nUse `gdb` for ANY debugger work ¥u2014 it's ‾10x more useful than¥n`gdb -batch` from bash because state persists.¥n¥nFor each response:¥n1. Include reasoning text explaining your analysis.¥n2. Provide ONE OR MORE tool calls (`bash` and/or `gdb`).¥n¥n## Workflow¥n¥n1. `bash`: read the source (`nl -ba program.c`).¥n2. `gdb`: `run` to observe the crash; `bt` for backtrace.¥n3. `gdb`: `frame N` / `print VAR` / `info locals` to inspect state.¥n4. Iterate as needed ¥u2014 `gdb` keeps your breakpoints / current frame.¥n5. Form a diagnosis grounded in the evidence.¥n¥n3-8 investigation steps is typical.¥n¥n## CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS¥n¥n- Every response MUST include AT LEAST ONE tool call (`bash` or¥n  `gdb`). A response with no tool calls is rejected.¥n- Use non-interactive flags. No editors / pagers (`vi`, `less`).¥n- Each `bash` call is a fresh subshell; `cd` and `export` don't¥n  persist between bash calls. The `gdb` session, by contrast, IS¥n  stateful.¥n¥n## Submission¥n¥nWhen done investigating, your final response MUST include all three¥nlabelled paragraphs in the THOUGHT text BEFORE the submit `bash` call:¥n¥n  ROOT CAUSE: <file:line and what is wrong, in your own words.>¥n  LOCAL FIX:  <minimal code change that resolves the symptom.>¥n  GLOBAL FIX: <structural change preventing this CLASS of bug ¥u2014¥n              type changes, API redesign, compile-time check, etc.>¥n¥nThen submit using:¥n¥n    bash tool: {¥

}¥n¥nThe judge reads your THOUGHT prose. If you submit without writing the¥nthree labelled sections, your run scores 0.¥n¥n<system_information>¥nLinux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2 x86_64¥n</system_information>¥n</instructions>
